claim_text: Alirocumab treatment reduces apo(a) fractional clearance rate.

runs.response_text:
VERDICT: CONTRADICT

The abstract for document ID 6540064 reports that alirocumab treatment led to a tendency of increased fractional clearance rate (FCR) of apo(a) (24.6%) without any change in its production rate. This directly contradicts the claim that alirocumab reduces the apo(a) fractional clearance rate.

VERDICT: SUPPORT
The abstract directly supports the claim by stating that alirocumab treatment reduces the fractional clearance rate (FCR) of apo(a) in human participants. The study found that alirocumab decreased LDL-apoB by increasing LDL-apoB FCR and decreasing LDL-apoB PR, indicating a reduction in apo(a) FCR. This finding aligns with the claim that alirocumab treatment reduces apo(a) FCR.

VERDICT: CONTRADICT

The abstract explicitly states that "the FCR of apo(a) tended to increase (24.6%) without any change in apo(a) PR" during alirocumab treatment, directly contradicting the claim that alirocumab reduces the apo(a) fractional clearance rate.

VERDICT: SUPPORT
The abstract states that "Alirocumab had no effects on FCRs or PRs of very low-density lipoproteins-apoB and very low-density lipoproteins triglycerides or on postprandial plasma triglycerides or apoB48 concentrations. The increase in apo(a) FCR during alirocumab treatment suggests that increased LDL receptors may also play a role in the reduction of plasma Lp(a)." This directly supports the claim that Alirocumab treatment reduces apo(a) fractional clearance rate.

VERDICT: CONTRADICT

The abstract reports that "the FCR of apo(a) tended to increase (24.6%) without any change in apo(a) PR" during alirocumab treatment, indicating that the fractional clearance rate of apo(a) actually rose, not reduced, contrary to the claim.

VERDICT: SUPPORT
The abstract from the PCSK9 inhibition study (doc_id 6540064) directly supports the claim that Alirocumab treatment reduces apo(a) fractional clearance rate. The results section states that "Alirocumab had no effects on FCRs or PRs of very low-density lipoproteins-apoB and very low-density lipoproteins triglycerides or on postprandial plasma triglycerides or apoB48 concentrations. The increase in apo(a) FCR during alirocumab treatment suggests that increased LDL receptors may also play a role in the reduction of plasma Lp(a)." This directly indicates that Alirocumab treatment increases the fractional clearance rate of apo(a), supporting the claim.
